PixiJS V5 Tutorial (38) - Packing Game Assets with TexturePacker
Most games pack a bunch of images into one big image, then run them through a tool to generate a JSON file. The whole point is to cut down on how many times the browser has to load assets.
TexturePacker is a dev tool built specifically for handling game assets —

. If you break the name apart it's basically Texture + Packer, and the nice thing is it takes a whole image sheet and spits out a JSON file for you, so you don't have to sit there calculating coordinates by hand.
Once you've downloaded it, go ahead and open TexturePacker (the free version is fine), then leave it running off to the side — we'll use it in a moment.
Next, the official site provides a
The image you download from the official site might come as a
To save you the hassle, here's
so you've got something to practice with.
Now we need to import those sequential images into TexturePacker. To do that, click "Add sprites" up top.
Once they're imported, you can actually select the whole set and hit preview — you'll see the image animate. I won't bother showing that here.

After that you just hit Publish sprite sheet. But there's one thing I'd recommend tweaking first: on the right side you need to pick a framework, and since we're using PixiJS, make sure you change this section.
Once you've confirmed everything looks good, hit Publish — and it'll generate the sprite sheet (a.k.a. the sprite atlas) along with the JSON.

The rest of the steps aren't really any different, so I won't go through the code in detail here — I'll just drop the result. (The JSON file is too big to paste anywhere, so I had to throw it on CodePen.)
See the Pen PIXI TexturePacker Sprite by Ray (@hsiangfeng) on CodePen.

One more thing — TexturePacker actually has a Chinese localization. Just click "Preferences" up top, find "Language" near the bottom, change it to "Chinese", then restart TexturePacker and you're done.

Most games cram a bunch of images into one big sheet and use a tool to generate a JSON file, which cuts down on load requests. This post walks through TexturePacker — installing it, importing your sprites, exporting a sprite sheet plus JSON for PixiJS, and even switching the UI to Chinese.
